questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon the insertion of an iron core with a cross-sectional area of $4 \text{ cm}^2$, the magnetic field inside a long solenoid having $50$ turns cm$^{-1}$ increases from $2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ to $2.5 \text{ T}$. Determine the magnetization $I$ of the core.
questions.solution:
The initial magnetic field inside the solenoid without the core is given by:

$B_0 = \mu_0 H = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$

The magnetic field after the insertion of the iron core is:

$B = \mu_0 (H + I) = 2.5 \text{ T}$

From the above equations, we can write:

$B = B_0 + \mu_0 I$

Rearranging for the magnetization $I$:

$I = \dfrac{B - B_0}{\mu_0}$

Substituting the given values:

$I = \dfrac{2.5 - 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}$

Since $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is very small compared to $2.5$, we can approximate $B - B_0 \approx 2.5 \text{ T}$:

$I \approx \dfrac{2.5}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}}$

$I \approx \dfrac{2.5}{12.56 \times 10^{-7}}$

$I \approx 1.99 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get:

$I \approx 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the centre of a long solenoid carrying a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$, the magnetic intensity $H$ is measured to be $1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. When a rod is inserted as the core in the solenoid, determine the magnetic intensity $H$ at the centre.
questions.solution:
The magnetic intensity $H$ inside a long solenoid is given by the formula $H = nI$, where $n$ is the number of turns per unit length and $I$ is the current flowing through the solenoid.

The value of magnetic intensity $H$ depends only on the free current $I$ and the geometry of the solenoid ($n$). It is completely independent of the medium or the material of the core inserted inside the solenoid.

Since the current and the number of turns per unit length remain unchanged, the magnetic intensity $H$ will remain the same after the rod is inserted.

Thus, the magnetic intensity $H = 1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the centre of a long solenoid carrying a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$, the magnetic intensity $H$ is measured to be $1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. A rod is inserted as the core in the solenoid, and the magnetization $I$ of the core is found to be $0.12 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. Identify whether the material of the rod is paramagnetic, diamagnetic, or ferromagnetic.
questions.solution:
The magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ of a material is defined as the ratio of its magnetization $I$ to the magnetic intensity $H$.

$\chi = \dfrac{I}{H}$

Substituting the given values:

$\chi = \dfrac{0.12}{1500} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$

Since the magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ is a small positive value, the material of the rod is paramagnetic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the centre of a long solenoid carrying a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$, the magnetic intensity $H$ is measured to be $1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. A rod is inserted as the core in the solenoid. Given that the magnetization $I$ of the core is $0.12 \text{ A m}^{-1}$, calculate the susceptibility of the material of the rod.
questions.solution:
The magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ of a material is defined as the ratio of the magnetization $I$ to the magnetic intensity $H$.

The given values are $H = 1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$ and $I = 0.12 \text{ A m}^{-1}$.

Using the relation for magnetic susceptibility:
$\chi = \dfrac{I}{H}$

Substituting the values:
$\chi = \dfrac{0.12}{1500}$

$\chi = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-2}}{15 \times 10^{2}}$

$\chi = 0.8 \times 10^{-4} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet, having a length of $1 \text{ cm}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1.0 \text{ cm}^2$, generates a magnetic field of $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ at an end-on position located $15 \text{ cm}$ from its centre. Determine the magnetic moment $M$ of the magnet.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at an axial (end-on) position of a short bar magnet is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3}$

Since the length of the magnet $l = 1 \text{ cm}$ is much smaller than the distance $d = 15 \text{ cm}$, the short magnet approximation is valid.

Substituting the given values:

$1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2M}{(0.15)^3}$

$M = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.15)^3}{2 \times 10^{-7}}$

$M = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^3 \times 0.003375}{2}$

$M = \dfrac{5.0625}{2}$

$M \approx 2.5 \text{ A m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long solenoid carries a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$, and the magnetic intensity $H$ at its centre is determined to be $1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. Calculate the number of turns per centimetre of the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The magnetic intensity $H$ at the centre of a long solenoid is given by:

$H = nI$

where $n$ is the number of turns per unit length and $I$ is the current.

Given $H = 1500 \text{ A m}^{-1}$ and $I = 2.0 \text{ A}$.

Substituting the values:

$1500 = n \times 2.0$

$n = 750 \text{ turns m}^{-1}$

To find the number of turns per centimetre, we divide by $100$:

$n = \dfrac{750}{100} = 7.5 \text{ turns cm}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $300 \text{ K}$, the susceptibility of magnesium is $1.2 \times 10^{-5}$. Determine the temperature at which the susceptibility increases to $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$.
questions.solution:
According to Curie's law for paramagnetic materials, the magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature $T$.

$\chi \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$

$\chi_1 T_1 = \chi_2 T_2$

Substituting the given values:

$1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 300 = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times T_2$

$T_2 = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 300}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 300 = 200 \text{ K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose each iron atom possesses a permanent magnetic moment of $2$ Bohr magnetons (where $1$ Bohr magneton equals $9.27 	imes 10^{-24} 	ext{ A m}^2$). The density of atoms in iron is given as $8.52 	imes 10^{28} 	ext{ atoms m}^{-3}$. Determine the maximum magnetic field $B$ on the axis inside a long cylinder of iron.
questions.solution:
The maximum magnetization $M$ is achieved when all atomic magnetic moments are aligned parallel to each other.

$M = n m$

Substituting the given values:

$n = 8.52 \times 10^{28} \text{ m}^{-3}$

$m = 2 \times 9.27 \times 10^{-24} \text{ A m}^2 = 1.854 \times 10^{-23} \text{ A m}^2$

$M = (8.52 \times 10^{28}) \times (1.854 \times 10^{-23}) \approx 1.58 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$

For a long cylinder, the demagnetizing factor is zero, so the maximum magnetic field on the axis is given by $B = \mu_0 M$.

$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.58 \times 10^6$

$B = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 0.158 \approx 1.98 \text{ T}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get $B \approx 2.0 \text{ T}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A permanent magnet has a coercive force of $4.0 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-1}$. It is inserted into a long solenoid having $40$ turns/cm. To completely demagnetize the magnet, a current is sent through the solenoid. Determine this current.
questions.solution:
The coercive force $H_c$ is the magnetic field intensity required to completely demagnetize the magnet.

For a long solenoid, the magnetic field intensity $H$ is given by $H = nI$, where $n$ is the number of turns per unit length and $I$ is the current.

Given:
$H_c = 4.0 \times 10^4 \text{ A m}^{-1}$
$n = 40 \text{ turns/cm} = 4000 \text{ turns/m}$

Equating $H$ to $H_c$ to find the required current:
$nI = H_c$

$4000 \times I = 4.0 \times 10^4$

$I = \dfrac{40000}{4000} = 10 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon the insertion of an iron core with a cross-sectional area of $4 \text{ cm}^2$, the magnetic field inside a long solenoid having $50$ turns cm$^{-1}$ increases from $2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ to $2.5 \text{ T}$. Determine the current flowing in the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long solenoid without a core is given by $B_0 = \mu_0 n I$.

Given:
Initial magnetic field, $B_0 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$
Number of turns per unit length, $n = 50 \text{ cm}^{-1} = 5000 \text{ m}^{-1}$
Permeability of free space, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$

Substituting the values into the formula:
$2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5000 \times I$

$2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 20000\pi \times 10^{-7} \times I$

$2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times I$

$I = \dfrac{2.5}{2\pi} \approx \dfrac{2.5}{6.28} \approx 0.4 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon the insertion of an iron core with a cross-sectional area of $4 \text{ cm}^2$, the magnetic field inside a long solenoid having $50$ turns cm$^{-1}$ increases from $2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ to $2.5 \text{ T}$. Determine the pole strength developed in the core.
questions.solution:
Given:
Initial magnetic field, $B_0 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$
Final magnetic field, $B = 2.5 \text{ T}$
Cross-sectional area, $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

The magnetic field inside the solenoid without the core is $B_0 = \mu_0 H$.
With the iron core inserted, the magnetic field becomes $B = \mu_0 (H + I)$, where $I$ is the intensity of magnetization of the core.

Therefore, $B = B_0 + \mu_0 I$
$\Rightarrow I = \dfrac{B - B_0}{\mu_0}$

Since $B_0 \ll B$, we can approximate $B - B_0 \approx B = 2.5 \text{ T}$.

The pole strength $m$ developed in the core is given by:
$m = I \times A \approx \dfrac{B}{\mu_0} \times A$

Substituting the given values:
$m = \dfrac{2.5}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}$

$m = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^3}{\pi} = \dfrac{2500}{\pi} \approx 795.8 \text{ A m}$

The closest value among the given options is $800 \text{ A m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At saturation, the susceptibility of annealed iron is $5500$. Determine the permeability of annealed iron at saturation.
questions.solution:
Given magnetic susceptibility, $\chi = 5500$

The relative permeability is given by:

$\mu_r = 1 + \chi$

$\mu_r = 1 + 5500 = 5501$

The permeability of the material is:

$\mu = \mu_r \mu_0$

Substituting the values:

$\mu = 5501 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$

$\mu = 5501 \times 4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}$

$\mu \approx 6.9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet with a length of $1 \text{ cm}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1.0 \text{ cm}^2$ creates a magnetic field of $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ at a point in the end-on position, located $15 \text{ cm}$ away from its centre. Calculate the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the magnet.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the end-on position of a short bar magnet is $B_{axial} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3}$.

Substituting the given values:

$1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2M}{(0.15)^3}$

$M = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.003375}{2 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.53 \text{ A m}^2$

The volume of the magnet is $V = A \times l = (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

The magnetization $I$ is:

$I = \dfrac{M}{V} = \dfrac{2.53}{10^{-6}} = 2.53 \times 10^6 \text{ A/m}$

The pole strength $m$ is:

$m = \dfrac{M}{l} = \dfrac{2.53}{10^{-2}} = 253 \text{ A m}$

The magnetic intensity $H$ at the centre due to the two poles (at a distance $l/2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$ each) is directed opposite to the magnetization:

$H = -2 \times \dfrac{1}{4\pi} \dfrac{m}{(l/2)^2} = -\dfrac{2 \times 253}{4\pi(0.005)^2} = -\dfrac{506}{\pi \times 10^{-4}} \approx -1.61 \times 10^6 \text{ A/m}$

The magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the magnet is:

$B = \mu_0(I + H) = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (2.53 \times 10^6 - 1.61 \times 10^6)$

$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 0.92 \times 10^6 \approx 1.16 \text{ T} \approx 1.2 \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet has a length of $1 \text{ cm}$ and a cross-sectional area of $1.0 \text{ cm}^2$. It generates a magnetic field of $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ at a point in the end-on position, $15 \text{ cm}$ away from its centre. Determine the magnetization $I$ of the magnet.
questions.solution:
Given, length of the magnet, $2l = 1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
Cross-sectional area, $A = 1.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Magnetic field at end-on position, $B = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$
Distance from the centre, $d = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$

Since $d \gg 2l$, the magnetic field at the end-on (axial) position of a short bar magnet is given by:
$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{d^3}$

Substituting the given values:
$1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{2M}{(0.15)^3}$

$M = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.375 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-7}}$

$M \approx 2.53 \text{ A m}^2$

The volume of the magnet is:
$V = A \times 2l = 10^{-4} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

The magnetization $I$ is defined as the magnetic moment per unit volume:
$I = \dfrac{M}{V}$

$I = \dfrac{2.53}{10^{-6}} = 2.53 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, we get $2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose each iron atom possesses a permanent magnetic moment of $2$ Bohr magnetons (where $1$ Bohr magneton equals $9.27 	imes 10^{-24} 	ext{ A m}^2$). The density of atoms in iron is given as $8.52 	imes 10^{28} 	ext{ atoms m}^{-3}$. Determine the maximum magnetization $I$ in a long cylinder of iron.
questions.solution:
Maximum magnetization is achieved when all atomic magnetic moments are perfectly aligned parallel to each other.

The maximum magnetization $I$ is the total magnetic moment per unit volume, given by:

$I = n \times m$

where $n$ is the number of atoms per unit volume and $m$ is the magnetic moment of each atom.

Substituting the given values:

$I = (8.52 \times 10^{28}) \times (2 \times 9.27 \times 10^{-24})$

$I = 8.52 \times 18.54 \times 10^4$

$I = 157.96 \times 10^4$

$I \approx 1.58 \times 10^6 \text{ A m}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a material, the magnetic field $B$ and the magnetic intensity $H$ are observed to be $1.6 \text{ T}$ and $1000 \text{ A m}^{-1}$, respectively. Determine the relative permeability $\mu_r$ and the susceptibility $\chi$ of the material.
questions.solution:
The relation between magnetic field $B$, magnetic intensity $H$, and relative permeability $\mu_r$ is given by:

$B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$

$\mu_r = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 H}$

Substituting the given values, $B = 1.6 \text{ T}$, $H = 1000 \text{ A m}^{-1}$, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$:

$\mu_r = \dfrac{1.6}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^4}{4\pi}$

$\mu_r = \dfrac{4000}{\pi} \approx 1273.2 \approx 1.3 \times 10^3$

The magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ is related to relative permeability by:

$\chi = \mu_r - 1$

$\chi \approx 1273.2 - 1 = 1272.2 \approx 1.3 \times 10^3$
